items.question: If it ___ tomorrow, I'll stay at home.
explanations: The if-clause never uses 'will' in the first conditional.
Correct! The condition uses the present simple: 'If it rains'.
'Rained' is past simple, used in the second conditional, not here.
'Is raining' is present continuous (now), not this future condition.

items.question: If you study hard, you ___ the exam.
explanations: 'Pass' (present simple) is for the if-clause, not the result.
Correct! The result of a first conditional uses 'will': 'you'll pass'.
'Passed' is past simple, used in the second conditional.
'Would pass' belongs to the second conditional, not this real future.

items.question: If I ___ time, I'll call you later.
explanations: The if-clause never uses 'will' in the first conditional.
Correct! The condition uses the present simple: 'If I have time'.
'Had' is past simple, which belongs to the second conditional.
'Would have' belongs to the third conditional.

items.question: If we leave now, we ___ the train.
explanations: 'Catch' (present simple) is for the if-clause, not the result.
Correct! The result uses 'will': 'we'll catch the train'.
'Caught' is past simple, used in the second conditional.
'Would catch' belongs to the second conditional.

items.question: If you don't hurry, you ___ the bus.
explanations: Correct! A warning about a real future uses 'will': 'you'll miss the bus'.
'Miss' (present simple) is for the if-clause, not the result.
'Missed' is past simple, used in the second conditional.
'Would miss' belongs to the second conditional.

items.question: If she ___ me, I'll help her.
explanations: The if-clause never uses 'will' in the first conditional.
Correct! The condition uses the present simple: 'If she asks me'.
'Asked' is past simple, used in the second conditional.
'Would ask' belongs to the second conditional.

items.question: If you finish early, you ___ go home.
explanations: 'Will' is possible, but 'can' fits 'be allowed to go home' more naturally here.
Correct! The result can use a modal like 'can': 'you can go home'.
'Would' belongs to the second conditional.
'Could have' belongs to the third conditional.

items.question: Correct this: 'If it will be sunny, we'll go to the beach.'
options: If it's sunny, we'll go to the beach.
If it will sunny, we go to the beach.
If it is sunny, we go to the beach.
If it will be sunny, we go to the beach.
explanations: Correct! The if-clause uses the present simple: 'If it's sunny, we'll go to the beach'.
'If it will sunny' is missing a verb and still wrongly uses 'will'.
The result needs 'will': 'we'll go', not 'we go'.
The if-clause must not use 'will'.

items.question: Rewrite with the same meaning: 'Hurry up or you'll be late.'
options: If you hurry up, you'll be late.
If you don't hurry up, you'll be late.
If you hurried up, you'd be late.
You're late if you hurry up.
explanations: 'If you hurry up, you'll be late' reverses the meaning.
Correct! 'Hurry or you'll be late' = 'If you don't hurry, you'll be late.'
The second conditional ('hurried… you'd') changes it to an imaginary situation.
This changes the meaning and the logic.

items.question: I think it's possible. If I ___ enough money, I'll buy a car.
explanations: Correct! A real, likely future takes the present simple in the if-clause: 'If I save'.
'Saved' is the second conditional, for something imaginary or unlikely.
The if-clause never uses 'will'.
'Would save' belongs to the second conditional.
